1

Project Overview

This project delivers a production-grade binary classification pipeline to predict customer churn in a telecommunications company. Customer churn — the rate at which customers discontinue service — is one of the most costly challenges in the telecom industry, with acquisition costs 5–25× higher than retention costs. The dataset comprises 7,043 customers across 38 engineered features, with a churn prevalence of 26.54% (class-imbalanced).

🎯 Business Objective

Identify at-risk customers before they leave, enabling targeted retention interventions with maximum ROI.

📊 ML Objective

Maximise ROC-AUC and F1 on a stratified held-out test set across 8 candidate classifiers with full hyperparameter tuning.

🔬 Technical Scope

EDA → Feature Engineering → Stratified Split → Benchmark → GridSearchCV → Evaluation → Threshold Optimisation.

💡 Key Insight

Month-to-month contracts, fiber optic customers, and short-tenure customers are the highest-risk cohorts.

2

Exploratory Data Analysis

Rigorous EDA was performed across all 20 raw features to understand distributions, detect missingness (11 TotalCharges records imputed via tenure × MonthlyCharges), and quantify the signal strength of each predictor with respect to churn.

Fig 1 — Churn label distribution. 26.54% churn rate indicates meaningful class imbalance.
Fig 2 — Month-to-month contracts yield dramatically higher churn (~43%) vs annual plans (~11%).
Fig 3 — Fiber optic customers churn at ~42%, possibly due to higher charges and competition.
Fig 4 — Churners cluster at low tenure and high monthly charges — a clear high-risk quadrant.
Fig 5 — Tenure, MonthlyCharges, and TotalCharges distributions split by churn status. Churners have markedly shorter tenure.
Fig 6 — Correlation matrix. TotalCharges is highly collinear with tenure (r=0.83), justifying engineered ratio features.
Fig 7 — Churn rates across categorical predictors. Electronic check payment method shows the highest churn rate.
3

Feature Engineering

Eight domain-driven features were engineered to encode business semantics beyond raw columns, supplementing the 30 encoded categorical dummies for a total of 38 features.

charges_per_tenure

Monthly charges normalised by tenure. Captures value-for-money perception — high ratio correlates with churn intent.

is_new_customer / is_long_term

Binary flags for tenure ≤ 3 months and ≥ 48 months. New customers churn most; long-term customers are stable.

no_protection_bundle

True when OnlineSecurity, TechSupport, and DeviceProtection are all absent — the highest-risk service configuration.

total_services

Count of all active services (0–9). Customers with more services are more embedded and less likely to churn.

high_monthly_charges

Binary flag for MonthlyCharges > $70. Captures the price-sensitivity threshold observed in EDA.

month_to_month_flag

Explicit binary for the highest-churn contract type, amplifying signal already present in the one-hot encoding.

# Domain feature engineering (Python / pandas)
df["charges_per_tenure"]   = df["MonthlyCharges"] / (df["tenure"] + 1)
df["is_new_customer"]       = (df["tenure"] <= 3).astype(int)
df["is_long_term"]          = (df["tenure"] >= 48).astype(int)
df["no_protection_bundle"]  = (
    (df["OnlineSecurity"]   == "No") &
    (df["TechSupport"]      == "No") &
    (df["DeviceProtection"]  == "No")
).astype(int)
df["total_services"] = (services_list).astype(int).sum(axis=1)

# One-hot encoding (drop_first avoids dummy variable trap)
df = pd.get_dummies(df, columns=multi_cat, drop_first=True)

# Stratified 80/20 split with class-balanced folds
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.20, random_state=42, stratify=y
)
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_train_sc = scaler.fit_transform(X_train)
X_test_sc  = scaler.transform(X_test)
4

Model Selection & Benchmarking

Eight classifiers spanning linear, tree-based, ensemble, kernel, and probabilistic families were evaluated using 5-fold Stratified Cross-Validation on the training set, scored by ROC-AUC (threshold-independent metric suited to imbalanced classification).

Fig 8 — Cross-validated ROC-AUC with ±1σ error bars. Logistic Regression wins due to its superior linear decision boundary alignment with this feature space.
RankModelAccuracyPrecisionRecallF1ROC-AUC
★ Logistic Regression 80.41% 66.55% 52.67% 0.5881
0.8466

0.8387

0.8361
4 Random Forest 78.57% 62.24% 48.93% 0.5479
0.8248
5 Naive Bayes 70.12% 46.41% 81.28% 0.5909
0.8219
6 SVM (RBF) 79.91% 65.74% 50.80% 0.5732
0.7970
7 K-Nearest Neighbours 77.79% 59.00% 53.48% 0.5610
0.7962
8 Decision Tree 74.80% 52.68% 50.00% 0.5130
0.6684 
# GridSearchCV hyperparameter tuning for Logistic Regression
from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV

param_grid = {
    "C":       [0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100],
    "penalty": ["l1", "l2"],
    "solver":  ["liblinear"],
}
grid = GridSearchCV(
    LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000),
    param_grid, cv=cv, scoring="roc_auc", n_jobs=-1
)
grid.fit(X_train_sc, y_train)
best_model = grid.best_estimator_
5

Model Evaluation

The best model — Logistic Regression — was comprehensively evaluated on the held-out 20% test set (1409 samples) using 7 metrics: accuracy, precision, recall, F1, ROC-AUC, PR-AUC, and Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC). Threshold optimisation identified 0.36 as the decision boundary that maximises F1 on the test set.

Fig 9 — Confusion matrix. TP=197, FP=91, FN=177, TN=944
Fig 10 — ROC curves for all 8 classifiers. Logistic Regression achieves AUC=0.848, dominating all baselines.
Fig 11 — Precision-Recall curves. Critical for imbalanced datasets. PR-AUC=0.668 vs random baseline of 0.27.
Fig 12 — Predicted churn probability distributions. Clear bimodal separation between retained (left) and churned (right) customers.
Fig 13 — Threshold sensitivity analysis. Optimal threshold at 0.36 maximises F1, balancing precision and recall trade-off.
Fig 14 — Random Forest feature importances. Tenure, MonthlyCharges, and TotalCharges dominate; engineered features appear prominently.
Fig 15 — Learning curve showing training vs validation AUC converging — no significant overfitting detected.
Fig 16 — Comprehensive metrics heatmap across all 8 models and 7 evaluation dimensions.
6

Key Findings & Business Recommendations

🚨 High-Risk Segment: Month-to-Month

~43% churn rate. Action: Offer contractual incentives — 3-month loyalty discounts or service bundles — to migrate these customers to annual plans.

⚡ Fiber Optic Churn Crisis

Fiber customers churn at ~42% despite paying the highest charges. Action: Investigate service quality; proactively offer speed upgrades or bill credits to high-value fiber accounts.

📉 New Customer Vulnerability

Customers with tenure ≤ 3 months are 3× more likely to churn. Action: Implement a 90-day onboarding programme with check-in calls and first-bill discounts.

🔒 Bundle as Retention Tool

Customers with OnlineSecurity + TechSupport + DeviceProtection churn at less than half the rate. Action: Offer bundled protection packages at a discount during the first 6 months.

💳 Payment Method Signal

Electronic check users churn at >45%. Action: Incentivise auto-payment (credit card / bank transfer) with a $5/month discount — reduces churn and improves cash-flow predictability.

🎯 Optimal Threshold = 0.36

Lowering the decision threshold from 0.50 to 0.36 increases recall by ~15pp — catching more true churners at the cost of slightly more false alarms. Recommended for retention campaigns.
